content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 20, 2006

Michael Reiter, Assistant City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
City of Redlands
P. O. Box 3005
Redlands, CA 92373-1505

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-117

Dear Mr. Reiter:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Planning Commissioner Gary Miller regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your letter provides no facts as to specific decisions or financial interests and so we provide only general advice.² In addition, the Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTION

Is there a waiting period under the Act which Planning Commissioner Gary Miller must observe between making a governmental decision to approve a project and then providing paid services to the person whose project was approved?

CONCLUSION

There is nothing in the Act regarding such a waiting period.

_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-06-117
Page No. 2


FACTS

Planning Commissioner Gary Miller is an architect practicing in the City of
Redlands. Recently, as a planning commissioner, he approved a project. He would like
to know if there is a waiting period under the Act which Planning Commissioner Gary
Miller must observe before contracting to provide paid services to the person whose
project was approved.


ANALYSIS³

The Act does not bar a public official from holding any form of private-sector
employment, nor does it impose any time limit on seeking private employment after
participating in some governmental action concerning a potential employer. However,
the Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit (with limited exceptions) any public official
from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he or she has a financial interest. (Sections
87100 and 87103.)

In applying this law, the Commission employs an eight-step analysis to assist in
determining whether a public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest.⁴
(Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) Regulation 18700(b) provides:

(1) Determine whether the individual is a public official, within the meaning of
the Act. (See section 82048; regulation 18701.) As a Planning Commissioner for the
City of Redlands, Commissioner Miller is considered a public official within the meaning
of the Act. (Section 87103, regulation 18701.)

(2) Determine whether the public official will be making, participating in
making, or using or attempting to use his/her official position to influence a government
decision. (See regulation 18702.)

(3) Identify the public official's economic interests. (See regulation 18703.)
There are six kinds of economic interests, defined in section 87103 and regulations
18703-18703.5, from which conflicts of interests may arise. Two are pertinent to your
question. The first one is Commissioner Miller's interest in any source of income,
including promised income, to him which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months

³ The Commission's advice is limited to matters arising under the Act. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(D).) You may wish to contact the state Attorney General's office with respect to other
provisions of law that might apply to your facts.

⁴ Enclosed is the Commission fact sheet entitled "Can I Vote? Overview of the Conflicts Laws,"
which details all eight steps for your future reference.

File No. 1-06-117
Page No. 3


prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3, emphasis added.)
Regulation 18703.3(a)(1) provides:

"A public official's income includes income which has been
promised to the public official but not yet received by him or her, if he or
she has a legally enforceable right to the promised income."

In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision foreseeably
resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, finances, or liabilities
of the official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a 12-
month period. (Regulation 18703.5.)

(4) For each of the public official's economic interests, determine whether that
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision which the public
official will be making, participating in making, or using or attempting to use his/her
official position to influence. (See regulation 18704.)

(5) Determine the applicable materiality standard for each economic interest,
based upon the degree of involvement determined pursuant to California Code of
Regulations, title 2, section 18704. (See regulation 18705.)

(6) Determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on the official's economic interests. If it is
not reasonably foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect on any of the
public official's economic interests, he or she does not have a conflict of interest within
the meaning of the Act.

(7) Determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. If the official can establish that the reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on his or her economic interest is indistinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, he or she does not have a conflict of interest
within the meaning of the Act. (See regulation 18707.)

(8) Determine if the public official's participation is legally required despite the
conflict of interest. (See regulation 18708.)

File No. I-06-117
Page No. 4


Because we have no further facts regarding this matter, we will stop the analysis
here. If you have any other questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature]
By: Adrianne Korchmares CCJ
Political Reform Consultant II

Enclosures

opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
#;

Because we have no further facts regarding this matter, we will stop the analysis
